column1: === LUKE â€” Speaker Metadata Coder ===
Primary role: SPEAKER METADATA for all 50 videos (speaker_name, speaker_type, speaker_gender)
No secondary role â€” metadata does not require IRR (factual lookup, not interpretive judgment)
Your assigned videos for SEGMENTATION: V26 V27 V28 V29 V30 V31 V32 V36
[SEG] = segment boundaries already identified (but NOT split into TCUs yet â€” still your job to split)
STEP-BY-STEP FOR YOUR ASSIGNED VIDEOS:
1. Open transcript at the ai_mention_timestamp
2. Scroll backward to find segment_start, forward to find segment_end
3. Read transcript between boundaries â†’ identify TCU breaks
4. For each TCU: fill tcu_id, tcu_transcript, tcu_start, tcu_end
5. IMMEDIATELY fill speaker_name, speaker_type, speaker_gender from meeting records/transcript
6. DELETE the original keyword-hit rows for that video once all TCUs are added
7. Post your updated file to the shared folder by end of day
FOR OTHER VIDEOS (not your assigned ones):
As other students post their TCU rows, fill in speaker_name, speaker_type,
speaker_gender for each new TCU row using meeting records and transcript context.
When you finish your part, you can help James for TCU identification.
METADATA DEFINITIONS:
speaker_role:official / public / vendor / other
speaker_gender: female / male / unknown (perceived binary, from name/appearance/pronouns only)
original_row_number
column9: tcu_transcript

column7: And then the other question related to Gavi, either… ...that is, you know, I feel like every other day I read an article about the Godfather of AI, saying we got to be careful, we should hit the brakes and slow down. And all this, and I can't help but think as we talk about these topics, Terminator and, you know, the pre-crime unit from these movie that I saw right here. All these things come to mind, right? And so, does Gavi, I, given that it's a broad coalition, I saw the map, the United States, it seems like it's all over the place, maybe, at East Coast, West Coast, with some other sprinkled in between. But are you all, or are we all taking a position as it relates to the cautious nature that some of these folks are preaching, that we should take as it relates to the advancement of AI? I will give you my personal opinion, and I'll lead the team chime in if they want. If you look at the technologies introduced in the last 30 years, and this is actually one of my slides when I showed all the advancements in AI, and if you look at all the advancements and what these technologies brought to us, including the iPhone and social media and everything, and you multiply that by 10,000... We get close to the potential benefit of AI. At the same time, if you take all the concerns that we had because of social media, cybersecurity, privacy, and everything that we learned in the last 25 years, and you multiply that by the same factor, 10,000... We have the same concern about the risk. So it is benefit and risk, and my position is, I want to make sure that we are at the leading edge of understanding and driving that technology, but at the same time, we are as fearful as possible of that technology. To make sure that we are not implementing something that is going to come back and bite us. And one of the things that we discuss in the coalition, if one of us get a black eye because of AI, all of us will be tainted by the same color. After all, all of us are government employees, all of us are government agencies, so when something happens in New York related to chat, and I try to bring something to counsel to approve a chat, I am going to be tainted question by the mishaps of some other agencies. And that is really one of the beautiful things that we have with the coalition. That we are helping each other out to figure out what is going on, and how can we be in a better position to defend and identify potential risks as a government entity versus being fragmented into cities, counties, and states, because all of us are faced with the same challenge. And on that same line is Gove AI, because I think we have sort of lifted it up as we are doing all these wonderful things, which I am glad we are taking the lead, and bringing together all of these different government agencies. But is there a policy position or a statement of policies or whatnot that Gove AI, that the coalition has taken as a release to federal legislation looking at how AI is progressing and just the thoughtfulness of it and the policies? We caught the attention of the White House Office of Technology, the Governance Office, the Homeland Security, and they are really interested in the work that we are doing, especially that we are capable or able to attract great minds throughout the nation that are willing to volunteer their time and energy to work with us. So there is a lot of conversation happening where they are trying to understand from us what we are seeing as risks so they can implement certain policies at the federal and national levels, but they cannot move as fast as we can, just getting something passed. The federal level might take years and months, or what we are doing is really implementing things in days, weeks and months. So our template AI policy was established in four months, and when we got 150 agencies drafting that and agreeing that this is something that the olcan work would. If you try to do something at the federal level, instead of four months, it is going to be four years or even more. So that is why I think the balance between what the federal level is doing and what we are doing is very interesting to them that they see that a lot of what we are seeing can influence their policies and a lot of things that they are seeing can influence what we are trying to do in a quick way.

column7: such obstacle to clear before it becomes a massive issue we see the impact these software services ultimately have in multifamily housing across metro areas algorithmically generated recommendations keep unit prices pressed up against the price ceiling for local markets regardless of the state of the market or the tenants who have historically resided in those areas and it's the tenants that I have and many of us have been elected to protect tenants with landlords using these services pay more in rent than their neighbors who don't have a landlord or an AI co-pilot if you will that they just so happen to agree with 90% of the time and ultimately the fact that this is facilitated through the pooling of nonpublic competitor data is what actually got the US Department of Justice involved in its recently filed antitrust suit against these revenue management companies now is the right time to make this intervention in multifamily housing it's always the right time to do what's right remember that all of those apartment projects that moved forward when the tax abatement was winding down they're getting filled a new report put them at 65% occupied just on projects completed since 2021 I for one am not scared of a denser Philadelphia which that and other reports point to a real demand for however setting the right conditions for all of our residents to have true access as we get there matters deeply to me the central questions for me remain not should we build but how are we building what are we building and who are we building for it's my belief that this bill will help us answer those questions and demonstrate the value of local government getting ahead of big tech's tendency to scale first and then ask questions later

column7: The question was regarding the AI: is there a particular platform that is going to be encouraged for employees to utilize, and is there going to be a policy written? Because obviously, if they go out and use any platform, then it's out there and people may know. That’s a great question, and I’m glad you asked that, because we’re a municipality and we’re a government, so we can’t just incorporate AI and grab all of our data and who knows where it’s going. We have to put some really big gateways around everything. Our working group is working on a policy. I think there was another municipality—San Jose, was it, Max? Yes. So we are also members of the GovAI Coalition, which is a group of over 400 units of government who are working on standardizing policies and other things. And then, to that platform, we’re looking at adopting Microsoft Copilot. It’s not yet available for government clients, so if it was, we would probably want to be moving on that, but we’re not there yet, so it’s not even an option for us at this time. But that’s kind of where we’re going. One of the other chatbots that we’re looking at from a vendor is Citybot, and they’re known in municipal government. They’re the only municipal?government provider of AI?based chatbots, and several communities around us are using it—Buffalo Grove, Elgin. I’m very interested in learning more about that and maybe going with that. And that’s just AI for search, so what that does is it just crawls our data on our website to provide answers. But if we were to incorporate our own chatbot within the city, we would have to be careful that other information doesn’t leak—like, when somebody searches “Do I need a fence permit?”, they don’t get Geneva’s or something like that. So I would say we are at least a year or two off from really incorporating AI.

column7: Our next registered speaker is Herold Keller. Morning. Well, I'm here once again to beat a dead horse that hardly anybody cares about, but it's important to me, so I'm going to speak up about it even if nobody else cares. Really, PCTV — I was on the station for 15 years, as I previously stated, and, uh, as I previously stated, it didn't bother me about my show, I didn't bother them, so I didn't take any interest in learning about how nonprofit offices were supposed to work and why the station closed. But I've been trying to, since I was here two weeks ago, learn as much as I can about nonprofit, but there's so much conflicting information on the web about it and what people who have worked for nonprofits have told me. And I saw a 990 report that they have been losing money, so I wasn't questioning that they lost money, because they were losing their funding and all that kind of stuff, without getting into all those specifics of taxes and all that. But one interesting thing I did find, that I asked some people who work for nonprofits about — found it on Co?Pilot — which says in 2022 Pittsburgh Community Television Corporation allotted a significant amount of funding toward salaries, specifically 33.6% of her total expenses were dedicated in salaries, and $200 to $300,000 was going for salaries. The executive director salary was $888,000 a year. And for the 15 years I was there, every day, rumors, gossiping here, saying — I don't want to pay any attention to that, I don't want to get involved in any of that — but the typical rumors and gossip about the station keeping the funding for their salaries and all that kind of stuff, money not going to where it was supposed to. I said, well, I said to the other producers, “Well, they're not our bosses, we're not reporting to them. They need us more than we need them, so go to them, tell them your problems.” I mean, I don't — I'm not on the board of directors, whoever they were. You never saw them. They only had, like, one open meeting a year in January, and in that open meeting it was more or less small talk. No statistics or anything were presented about where the money was going. So it kind of made me suspicious, and I talked to some people who work for nonprofit, and they said, “You're right, go get them. They must have been doing something over there that was not kosher.” And I said, well, I don't want to accuse anybody of anything, since I have very little knowledge of nonprofit, but I'm just trying — I think for myself. I don't — nobody tells me what to think, as you could have probably imagined. And so I'm just looking for answers, and hopefully I can get some. Thank you. Thank you. Our last registered speaker is Ralph Williams.

column7: I would appreciate a chance to maybe go back. So we think of the data repository. Today it's largely structured database information. Policy information sort of gets at more unstructured data. And so unstructured data, being able to handle that better, is actually part of the — from a sort of how do we consume and analyze and use that information to answer questions. It's tied into, I would say, two of the requests we have put forward. One of them is the settlement agreement, the Public Safety Data Transformation. That Public Safety Data Transformation, one of the details of that is to try to incorporate unstructured data into a repository that would then be available for city employees, which is not something we have a large capacity around today. That, while it starts with public safety, our goal is to roll it out across all city departments and data sets, focused first because of the settlement agreement on safety. The other request that I'll be covering in the TIP presentation that comes to mind is the Co?Pilot AI tool. When I hear you talk about, like, how we are able to sort of process what is in the policies, having something like that tool may be a useful way to sort of surface that information to city employees. I think the main functionality that is in the policy management tools is really around the workflow and the versioning and the process of how we craft policies as opposed to how the city consumes that data. So then is the data repository an Azure instance or on?prem? Chair Chughtai, Council President Payne, today it is in our managed services environment. What is being proposed as part of the Public Safety Data Transformation project is to migrate it to a public government cloud infrastructure like Azure. Okay. Thank you.

column7: Councilwoman Deborah L. Gross presents Bill 765, report of the Committee on Innovation, Performance, and Asset Management and Technology for July 17th, 2024, with an affirmative recommendation. Bill 662, resolution amending Resolution 844 of 2023, authorizing the Mayor and the Director of the Department of Innovation and Performance and the Director of the Department of Mobility and Infrastructure on behalf of the City of Pittsburgh to enter into an amended agreement or agreements or amendments thereto with Cohen Law Group and CTC Technology and Energy for renegotiation of the city's cable franchise agreements with Comcast and Verizon and for consultation on development of fiber and wireless telecommunication assets and policy by increasing the approved amount by $60,000 for an amended total cost not to exceed $7,213.70 over five years. And Bill 663, resolution authorizing the Mayor and Department of Parks and Recreation on behalf of the City of Pittsburgh to enter into an agreement or agreements with Information Age Technologies Inc. for the Co?Pilot annual subscription fee for the Healthy Active Living Senior Center software subscription used for data collection and the report of services to Allegheny County Department of Human Services Area Agency on Aging as mandated by the city's contract with Allegheny County for a three?year term. The total amount for 2024 will be at a cost not to exceed $221,000, with future annual amounts to be negotiated on an as?needed basis. You have heard the reading and the title of the bill. Is there any discussion? If none, the bill is now ready for final action. All in favor to pass the bill will vote aye when their name is called. Those opposed will vote no. Mr. Lavelle, President, aye. Nine ayes. The bill having received the legally required number of votes is passed finally. That takes us to Councilman Kar Mosley presenting on the Committee on Intergovernmental and Educational Affairs.

column7: Good evening, members of council. Thank you for allowing me the time tonight to present the Department of Information Technologies FY25 budget. Before I get started, I just want to thank my wonderful staff for all the exceptional work that they do. And numerous hours that they work around the clock to make sure that the city is operational and secure and available to our employees constituents. So as always, we start off with an overview of the IT department. I did want to say before I started that I, thanks to our friends at Microsoft, I was able to get a little bit of a preview of Microsoft co-pilot, which is Microsoft's version of Generative AI similar to chat GPT. And so this presentation was completed with, you know, enhanced and completed with AI technologies. So this may be one of the first budget proposals. Let me budget a question, Director May is AI going to be giving the presentation. Yeah, yeah, but I am really here. I did. I've seen different things that are, is it going to be AI voice or? No, no, it's just AI was utilized to generate the person. You know in a future, maybe budget hearings will be. Possibly possibly. I was second. But if I say they're wrong tonight, then it's AI's fault not mine. Okay. So the IT department is composed of six units, which are listed there. Next slide please. So this is our mission statement. These are our core values. And these are strategic priorities for FY25.
